content_en:
Al-Hakim's methodology in the Mustadrak is one of the most debated topics in the classical hadith sciences. His stated project was to collect traditions meeting the standards of al-Bukhari and/or Muslim that the two masters had not included in their Sahihs. He assessed the isnads of the hadiths he included and pronounced on their authenticity, using formulas such as hadha hadith sahih al-isnad ala shart ash-Shaykhain (this tradition has a sound chain meeting the conditions of the two Shaykhs) or variations indicating that the chain met the standard of one of the two but not the other.

The critical problem with the Mustadrak is that al-Hakim's assessments were often demonstrably inconsistent. He authenticated traditions whose chains contained narrators assessed as unreliable by other major critics, and in some cases he authenticated narrations that are clearly fabricated by the standards of classical hadith criticism. This inconsistency led scholars to conclude that he either became less critical in his old age, that he was working too quickly to complete the project, or that his standards were genuinely less rigorous than he claimed.

Ad-Dhahabi, the great fourteenth-century hadith critic and historian, undertook the immense labor of abridging the Mustadrak and reviewing al-Hakim's authentication judgments for each hadith. His Talkhis al-Mustadrak was written as a companion to the text, and it is now conventionally printed alongside the Mustadrak itself. Ad-Dhahabi's review revealed that many of al-Hakim's authenticating judgments were correct but that a significant portion were wrong, including some he described as fabricated (mawdu). This work by ad-Dhahabi has become the standard guide for using the Mustadrak critically.

Despite these problems, the Mustadrak contains a large body of genuinely authenticated and valuable hadiths not found in the primary Sahihs. Scholars who know how to use it in conjunction with ad-Dhahabi's critique find it an invaluable resource. The challenge — and the educational opportunity — lies in learning to distinguish the reliable from the unreliable within a single large collection.
